coredata.dc:title: One-step solvothermal synthesis of red emissive carbonized polymer dots for latent fingerprint imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Feng
Wang, Xi
Liu, Wen
Wang, Luqi
Wang, Guiyan
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) have attracted much attention over the past decade due to their unique optical characteristics and negligible toxicity. However, it is very difficult to obtain red emission of CNMs. Bright red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were prepared by facilely adjusting the reaction temperature through a one-step solvothermal route, using p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as precursor. The as-synthesized CPDs exhibit excitation-independent emission at about 608 nm under excitation from 365 to 550 nm. Loading CPDs in starch to form CPDs–starch powder, the resultant powder exhibits strong red emission and the typical aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching was effectively suppressed, thus allowing the CPDs to be used in solid state form. More importantly, latent fingerprints (LFPs) on surfaces of various substrates, including glass, stainless steel, marble, adhesive tape, aluminum foil, painted wood, compact disc (CD), and coin, are developed by CPDs–starch powder and exhibit clear patterns and satisfactory ridge details under a 365 nm UV lamp. Furthermore, fingerprints on surfaces with multicolored background and strong florescent properties could also be clearly observed. These results suggest that CPDs–starch powder can be used as a fluorescent labeling agent for the visualization of LFPs in forensic science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Fluorescence imaging
Latent fingerprint detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the different CPDs samples.
(A) FTIR spectrum and (B) XPS survey spectrum of CPDs-160, (C, D) the corresponding expansion of the C1s and N1s peaks, respectively.
UV–vis absorption spectra of the different CPDs samples in ethanol.

Fluorescence excitation (A) and emission (B) spectra of CPDs prepared at different solvothermal temperatures, (C) emission spectra of CDs–160 ethanol solution at different excitation wavelengths, (D) emission spectra of CDs–160 in ethanol and CPDs-starch powder. Inset shows the photographs of CPDs-160 ethanol solution (right) and CPDs-starch powder (left) under 365 nm UV light.

Photographs of LFPs developed with CPDs-starch on (a) glass slide, (b) stainless steel (c) marble, (d) adhesive tape, (e) aluminum foil, (f) painted wood, (g) compact disc (CD), and (h) coin under 365 nm UV light, respectively.

Images of LFPs developed with CD-starch on glass (a), and magnified images of fingerprints with details (b) including bifurcation (1), crossover (2), core (3), and termination (4) under 365 nm UV irradiation.

Images of latent fingerprints developed with CPDs-starch powder on background interference substrates: (a) mobile charger, (b) membership card, (c) cigarette case, and (d) drug packing under 365 nm UV irradiation.
Illustration of a depletion series which consisted of eight consecutive fingerprints (a–h) on glass slides treated with CPDs-starch powder.
Photographs of CD-starch developed latent fingerprints on glass slides aged for different times: (a) 1 days, (b) 7 days, (c) 15 days, (d) 30 days.

head.ce:title: One-step solvothermal synthesis of red emissive carbonized polymer dots for latent fingerprint imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Normal College, Shenyang University, Shenyang, 110044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenyang No.27 High School, Shenyang, 110011, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, 110016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) have attracted much attention over the past decade due to their unique optical characteristics and negligible toxicity. However, it is very difficult to obtain red emission of CNMs. Bright red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were prepared by facilely adjusting the reaction temperature through a one-step solvothermal route, using p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as precursor. The as-synthesized CPDs exhibit excitation-independent emission at about 608 nm under excitation from 365 to 550 nm. Loading CPDs in starch to form CPDs–starch powder, the resultant powder exhibits strong red emission and the typical aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching was effectively suppressed, thus allowing the CPDs to be used in solid state form. More importantly, latent fingerprints (LFPs) on surfaces of various substrates, including glass, stainless steel, marble, adhesive tape, aluminum foil, painted wood, compact disc (CD), and coin, are developed by CPDs–starch powder and exhibit clear patterns and satisfactory ridge details under a 365 nm UV lamp. Furthermore, fingerprints on surfaces with multicolored background and strong florescent properties could also be clearly observed. These results suggest that CPDs–starch powder can be used as a fluorescent labeling agent for the visualization of LFPs in forensic science.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) including carbon nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots, and carbon dots (CDs), have attracted increasing attention due to their superior optical properties, high stability, low toxicity, excellent photoinduced electron transfer properties, and simple synthetic routes [1–3]. These characteristics make CNMs useful in a variety of applications, such as in bioimaging [4,5], light-emitting devices [6], photocatalysis [7], and so on. At present, various synthesis methods have been developed to prepare CNMs, including electrochemical oxidation, microwave synthesis, ultrasonic processing, thermal decomposition, and hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis strategies [8], etc. Meanwhile, organic molecules [9] and natural products [10] are successfully used as carbon precursors to prepare CNMs. The majority of current CNMs to date show intense blue light to green light emission under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light. However, synthesis of red fluorescent CNMs is very challenging, and lack of red-emitting CNMs greatly impedes their development and widespread applications.

To achieve red-emissive CDs, several methods have been developed, including surface state controlled by synthetic routes [11], element doping [12], using proper materials [13], and surface modification [14], etc. Ding et al. reported that multicolor emissive CDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method followed by column separation, used p-phenylenediamine and urea as starting materials [15]. Under 365 nm UV light excitation, these separated CDs emitted bright and stable emission in gradient colors from blue to red. The different emission colors were attributed to the different surface states. Miao et al. synthesized S, N codoped CDs using citric acid and thiourea as precursor by hydrothermal method [16]. Because of doping effect of S and N elements, the resulting CDs showed red emission at 594 nm with excitation at 560 nm. Jiang et al. fabricated red, green, and blue emission CDs using three isomers of phenylenediamine as precursor through a solvothermal process [17]. Through solvothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine in formamide solution, Ding et al. synthesized red-emitting CDs with emission peak at 627 nm when excited at 560 nm [18]. Experimental alteration and adjustment of surface chemistry of CDs with suitable functional groups have also been proved to be efficient ways to obtain red fluorescent CDs [19]. Despite huge efforts have been made to fabricate red-emissive CDs, the preparations involve complicated separation/purification process. Therefore, developing facile and controllable process to prepare CNMs with bright red emission is still highly desirable.

The developed red emissive CNMs can be used for fingerprint imaging which is useful for personal identification in forensic investigations. Most fingerprints deposited at crime scenes are invisible to naked eyes and are called latent fingerprints (LFPs). Various efforts by researchers in the development of new fluorescent reagents and methods for developing LFPs more efficiently have appeared [20,21]. Recently, available luminescent nanoparticles, especially rare earth fluorescent nanomaterials have been successfully used for the detection of LFPS [22–24]. CNMs, as one type of cost-effective, highly luminescent-stable, environmentally friendly nanomaterials, are also expected to be applied for the visualization of LFPS [25,26].

Here, we present a simple synthetic strategy to prepare red-emissive carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) by one-step solvothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine using toluene as solvent, which is carried out without any separation and purification process. By dispersing CPDs into starch matrix to prevent the fluorescence from quenching, this yielded powder produces bright red emission under 365 nm UV light excitation. The potential application of CPDs–starch powder for developing LFPs is explored. The results indicated that CPDs–starch powder can be employed as luminescent probes for imaging of LFPs with enhanced contrast, better selectivity, and low background interference on various surfaces.
p-Phenylenediamine (PPD, 99.9%), toluene, ethanol and starch obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China) were all of analytical grade.

XRD measurement was performed on an X'Pert Pro diffractometer (PANalytical Co., Holland) with Cu Kα radiation. The morphology of sample was observed by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was obtained on a Spectrum One FTIR spectrometer with the KBr pellet method (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded by the ESCALAB250 surface analysis system (Thermo VG Company, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on a UV-2100 spectrometer (Ruili, China). Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured on an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

CPDs were prepared by a one-step solvothermal method. Briefly, 0.20 g of p-phenylenediamine (PPD) was mixed with 20 mL of toluene within a Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 160 °C for 10 h in a constant temperature drying oven. After cooling down to room temperature, the crimson supernatant was discarded and the solids in the bottom of the autoclave were collected carefully and washed with toluene several times. For simplicity, the CPDs prepared at different reaction temperatures were denoted as CPDs-160, CPDs-180, and CPDs-210, respectively.

10 mg of CPDs was dissolved in 10 ml of ethanol to form a clear solution. Then, 1 g starch was added to the above solution with ultrasonic treatment and dried overnight under ambient circumstances to get a composite powder for red emission. The sample was denoted as CPDs-starch.

LFPs were collected from volunteers by lightly wiping fingers across forehead and then pressing fingers on a chosen substrate surfaces. To develop LFPs, CPDs-starch powder was dusted on the surface of substances followed by a light brushing action to eliminate excess powder. The developed LFPs were recorded with a Canon EOS 500D digital camera under UV 365 nm light.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the various CPDs samples. The presence of sharp and intense diffraction peaks revealed a highly ordered and crystalline structure of CPDs [11]. The X-ray data of obtained CPDs samples presents identical crystalline peaks, demonstrating that PPD can produce the process of successive polymerization and carbonization at the temperature range of 210 to 160 °C [27].
TEM image of as-prepared CPDs-160 is shown in Fig. 2 , which displays monodispersed carbon nanoparticles with average sizes about 12.5 nm.

FT-IR analysis was carried out to characterize the functional groups on CPDs-160 surface (Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3A, absorption peaks at the region of 3408–3201 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of NH group. Moreover, stretching vibrations of CN (1616 cm−1), CC (1516 cm−1), and CNC (1393 cm−1) bonds were observed. In addition, the characteristic absorption peak at 1265 cm−1 illustrated presence of NH functional group [28,29]. Then XPS spectra were collected to reveal surface elements and groups on the as-prepared CPDs-160. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3B) indicates that CPDs-160 mainly consist of carbon (C, 79.56%), nitrogen (N, 15.61%), and oxygen (O, 4.83%). The high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 3C) of C1s confirm existence of sp2 CC/CN bond (284.3 eV) and CN/CO bond (285.6 eV) [30]. The N1s spectrum presented in Fig. 3D could be split into three peaks 398.4, 399.3, and 400.2 eV, corresponding to CNC, NH, and CNC, respectively [31]. These new CNC, CNC, and NH groups appeared in CPDs-160, illustrating formation of polymer structures during the reaction process, which are also verified by XRD patterns.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the three selected samples, as shown in Fig. 4 , display analogous absorption in the UV–vis region of 200–600 nm. The three spectra exhibit three typical absorption bands at 244, 308, and 480 nm, which corresponded to π–π* transitions of CC and CN bonds, n–π* transition of the CN bond and other aromatic structure, and surface groups transition, respectively [32].

The fluorescence spectra of CPDs indicated that the excitation spectra had two main bands at 365 and 510 nm (Fig. 5 A), and optimal red emission peaks of CPDs synthesized at different solvothermal temperature were centered at 608 nm when excitation happened at 365 nm (Fig. 5B). It can be seen from Fig. 5B that the emission spectra of CPDs prepared at different temperatures yield two outstanding emission bands of blue and red light, and the relative intensity of red to blue are quite different. Until now, the mechanism for red emission of CDs has been unclear. According to previous results, origin of the blue emission is related to oxygenous-related defect states, while that of red emission comes from the nitrogen-containing fluorophores [33,34]. According to the results of XPS, oxygen content in CPDs-160 is only 4.83%, decreasing 3 fold as compared to previously reported red emission CDs based on PPD. By regulating solvothermal temperature from 210 °C to 160 °C, incorporated oxygen species in the CPDs structure are confined, while nitrogen-containing fluorophores are easily formed, leading to a decrease in blue emission but an increase in red emission, which indicates that the emission behavior of CPDs can be tuned by varying reaction temperature. Fig. 5C reveals the emission spectra of CPDs-160 in ethanol under excitation from 365 to 550 nm. PL emission peaks of CPDs-160 did not shift when excitation wavelength changed, which demonstrated the excitation-independent emission property of CPDs-160. The excitation-independent red emission of CPDs-160 can be explained based on documented reports ascribed to molecular states controlling PL features. Loading CDs into a solid matrix is a frequently used method to realize solid state luminescence. The intense red emission of CPDs in solid state was realized by dispersing CPDs in starch to form CD–starch powder, which overcame typical aggregation–induced fluorescence quenching, thus giving CPDs potential use toward fingerprint detection. Fig. 5D shows the emission spectra of CPDs-starch powder in contrast to corresponding CPDs ethanol solution. When CPDs are dispersed into starch, the emission spectrum of CPDs displays similar feature to that in ethanol. It can be observed that compared to solution, the emission peak of CPD-starch powder clearly shows a blue shift, which should be owing to the interaction of CPDs and starch [35].

The excellent solid-state red emission from CPDs-starch powder under 365 nm UV light was useful for latent fingerprint detection with low background interference and high contrast. The feasibility of CPDs-starch powder imaging method for fingerprints was evaluated on different substrates. Fig. 6 shows the fingerprint images developed by CPDs-starch powder on various substrate surfaces, such as glass slide, stainless steel, marble, adhesive tape, aluminum foil, painted wood, compact disc (CD), and coin (Fig. 6a-h). Fingerprints on all the developed surfaces exhibited clear images of well-defined ridges without background staining, with excellent brightness and contrast under 365 nm light irradiation. The results suggest that LFPs on various types of substrate can be smoothly detected using CPDs-starch powder.

Fig. 7 a shows a representative fluorescent image of LFPs visualized by staining CPDs-starch powder on a glass surface. Apparently, the clear and distinct ridge patterns of LFPs were displayed without any background hindrance. The magnified images from Fig. 7b also revealed that CPDs-starch powder was attached only to the ridges, and the grooves were empty and did not show any emission color. Furthermore, the expanded images allowed small features, including bifurcation, crossover, core, and termination, to be clearly distinguished by naked eye under 365 nm excitation, which are used to identify individuals (see Fig. 7b).

Optical imaging of LFPs has been widely used in forensic science, but background interference from the substrates may be frequently encouraged. Defining efficiency of red emitting CPDs-starch powder for the development of LFPs on various surfaces with background color and autofluorescence interference was investigated. The multiple background substrates including mobile charger, membership card, cigarette case, and drug box were chosen and LFPs were deposited on their surfaces. After CPDs-starch powder was applied on these surfaces, fluorescence images with sufficient contrast between background and red ridge can be obtained and details of the fingerprint ridges can be identified easily (Fig. 8a-d). The strong emission of CPDs-starch powder with long emission wavelength can provide high contrast and low interference for fluorescence imaging of LFPs. The results demonstrate that CPDs-starch powder can be used as an effective probe for development of LFPs on surfaces with background interference.

To further demonstrate the sensitivity of this method for practical use, a depletion series of fingerprints were deposited on surface of glass slides and developed with CPDs-starch powder. As shown in Fig. 9 , all fingerprints processed with CPDs-starch powder exhibited bright and clear ridge details, even the eighth fingerprint with least amount of secretion in series (Fig. 9h), indicating that CPDs-starch powder displays excellent sensitivity in latent fingerprint detection.

Fingerprints encountered in real situations are often aged rather than freshly deposited. Aging of fingerprints presents a significant challenge for LFPs detection because the loss of water, chloride ions, and other substances usually makes detection techniques ineffective [36]. As shown in Fig. 10 , all of aged fingerprints can be easily detected, and one can clearly see the finely resolved ridge details. It is worth noticing that even a 30 day old fingerprint is clearly observed, and details do not deteriorate as aging time increases. The above results suggest that CPDs-starch powder has favorable applicability and dependability as developing reagents for LFPs visualization.

In summary, bright red emissive CPDs were synthesized using p-phenylenediamine as a precursor in toluene via solvothermal method. The relative intensity of red to blue band strongly depends on reaction temperature. By dispersing CPDs into starch, solid-state luminescent CPDs-starch powder was successfully fabricated. Fingerprints on daily encountered substrates can be readily visualized using CPDs-starch powder, and obtained latent fingerprint images display bright, well-resolved ridge details. CPDs-starch powder can further be applied in detection of fingerprints on the surfaces with multiple background and florescent properties. Besides, fingerprints aged for long times were also easily detected. Due to their excellent photoluminescent properties and outstanding performance in latent fingerprint imaging, CPDs-starch powder can be considered as an effective and promising luminescent material for latent fingerprint detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the first “Green Valley Cup” young teachers' innovation and entrepreneurship project of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University.
